sentences.text: RISC-V was designed to provide a highly modular and extensible instruction set, and includes a large and growing set of standard extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: In addition, users may add their own custom extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: This flexibility can be used to highly optimize a specialized design by including only the exact set of ISA features required for an application, but the same flexibility also leads to a combinatorial explosion in possible ISA choices.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: Profiles specify a much smaller common set of ISA choices that capture the most value for most users, and which thereby enable the software community to focus resources on building a rich software ecosystem with application and operating system portability across different implementations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: NOTE: Another pragmatic concern is the long and unwieldy ISA strings required to encode common sets of extensions, which will continue to grow as new extensions are defined.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: Each profile is built on a standard base ISA plus a set of mandatory ISA extensions, and provides a small set of standard ISA options to extend the mandatory components.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: Profiles provide a convenient shorthand for describing the ISA portions of hardware and software platforms, and also guide the development of common software toolchains shared by different platforms that use the same profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: The intent is that the software ecosystem focus on supporting the profiles' mandatory base and standard options, instead of attempting to support every possible combination of individual extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: Similarly, hardware vendors should aim to structure their offerings around standard profiles to increase the likelihood their designs will have mainstream software support.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: NOTE: Profiles are not intended to prohibit the use of combinations of individual ISA extensions or the addition of custom extensions, which can continue to be used for more specialized applications albeit without the expectation of widespread software support or portability between hardware platforms.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: NOTE: As RISC-V evolves over time, the set of ISA features will grow, and new platforms will be added that may need different profiles.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: To manage this evolution, RISC-V is adopting a model of regular annual releases of new ISA profiles, following an ISA roadmap managed by the RISC-V Technical Steering Committee.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: The architecture profiles will also be used for branding and to advertise compatibility with the RISC-V standard.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: This volume describes the general structure of RISC-V architecture profiles and also the specifics of the officially defined profiles.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction

sentences.text: Profiles only describe ISA features, not a complete execution environment.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: A software platform is a specification for an execution environment, in which software targeted for that software platform can run.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: A hardware platform is a specification for a hardware system (which can be viewed as a physical realization of an execution environment).
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: Both software and hardware platforms include specifications for many features beyond details of the ISA used by RISC-V harts in the platform (e.g., boot process, calling convention, behavior of environment calls, discovery mechanism, presence of certain memory-mapped hardware devices, etc.).
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: Architecture profiles factor out ISA-specific definitions from platform definitions to allow ISA profiles to be reused across different platforms, and to be used by tools (e.g., compilers) that are common across many different platforms.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: A platform can add additional constraints on top of those in a profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: For example, mandating an extension that is a standard option in the underlying profile, or constraining some implementation-specific parameter in the profile to lie within a certain range.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: A platform cannot remove mandates or reduce other requirements in a profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: NOTE: A new profile should be proposed if existing profiles do not match the needs of a new platform.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Profiles versus Platforms

sentences.text: Every profile is a member of a profile family. A profile family is a set of profiles that share the same base ISA but which vary in highest-supported privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Family

sentences.text: The profile families defined in this volume are:
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Family

sentences.text: Generic unprivileged instructions (I) - Application processors running rich operating systems with binary software ecosystems (A) - Application processors running rich operating systems with rooms for customization (B)
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Family

sentences.text: NOTE: More profile families may be added over time.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Family

sentences.text: A profile family may be updated no more than annually, and the release calendar year is treated as part of the profile family name.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Family

sentences.text: Each profile family is described in more detail below.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Family

sentences.text: RISC-V has a layered architecture supporting multiple privilege modes, and most RISC-V platforms support more than one privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: Software is usually written assuming a particular privilege mode during execution.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: For example, application code is written assuming it will be run in user mode, and kernel code is written assuming it will be run in supervisor mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: NOTE: Software can be run in a mode different than the one for which it was written.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: For example, privileged code using privileged ISA features can be run in a user-mode execution environment, but will then cause traps into the enclosing execution environment when privileged instructions are executed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: This behavior might be exploited, for example, to emulate a privileged execution environment using a user-mode execution environment.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: The profile for a privilege mode describes the ISA features for an execution environment that has the eponymous privilege mode as the most-privileged mode available, but also includes all supported lower-privilege modes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: In general, available instructions vary by privilege mode, and the behavior of RISC-V instructions can depend on the current privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: For example, an S-mode profile includes U-mode as well as S-mode and describes the behavior of instructions when running in different modes in an S-mode execution environment, such as how an `ecall` instruction in U-mode causes a contained trap into an S-mode handler whereas an `ecall` in S-mode causes a requested trap out to the execution environment.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: A profile may specify that certain conditions will cause a requested trap (such as an `ecall` made in the highest-supported privilege mode) or fatal trap to the enclosing execution environment.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: The profile does not specify the behavior of the enclosing execution environment in handling requested or fatal traps.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: NOTE: In particular, a profile does not specify the set of ECALLs available in the outer execution environment.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: This should be documented in the appropriate binary interface to the outer execution environment (e.g., Linux user ABI, or RISC-V SEE).
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: NOTE: In general, a profile can be implemented by an execution environment using any hardware or software technique that provides compatible functionality, including pure software emulation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: A profile does not specify any invisible traps.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: NOTE: In particular, a profile does not constrain how invisible traps to a more-privileged mode can be used to emulate profile features.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: A more-privileged profile can always support running software to implement a less-privileged profile from the same profile family.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: For example, a platform supporting the S-mode profile can run a supervisor-mode operating system that provides user-mode execution environments supporting the U-mode profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: NOTE: Instructions in a U-mode profile, which are all executed in user mode, have potentially different behaviors than instructions executed in user mode in an S-mode profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: For this reason, a U-mode profile cannot be considered a subset of an S-mode profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

sentences.text: An architecture profile has a mandatory ratified base instruction set (RV32I or RV64I for the current profiles).
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: The profile also includes ratified ISA extensions placed into two categories:
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: As the name implies, Mandatory ISA extensions are a required part of the profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: Implementations of the profile must provide these.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: The combination of the profile base ISA plus the mandatory ISA extensions are termed the profile mandates, and software using the profile can assume these always exist.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: The Optional category (also known as options) contains extensions that may be added as options, and which are expected to be generally supported as options by the software ecosystem for this profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: NOTE: The level of "support" for an Optional extension will likely vary greatly among different software components supporting a profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: Users would expect that software claiming compatibility with a profile would make use of any available supported options, but as a bare minimum software should not report errors or warnings when supported options are present in a system.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: An optional extension may comprise many individually named and ratified extensions but a profile option requires all constituent extensions are present.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: In particular, unless explicitly listed as a profile option, individual extensions are not by themselves a profile option even when required as part of a profile option.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: For example, the Zbkb extension is not by itself a profile option even though it is a required component of the Zkn option.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: NOTE: Profile optional extensions are intended to capture the granularity at which the broad software ecosystem is expected to cope with combinations of extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: All components of a ratified profile must themselves have been ratified.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: Platforms may provide a discovery mechanism to determine what optional extensions are present.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: Extensions that are not explicitly listed in the mandatory or optional categories are termed non-profile extensions, and are not considered parts of the profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: Some non-profile extensions can be added to an implementation without conflicting with the mandatory or optional components of a profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: In this case, the implementation is still compatible with the profile even though additional non-profile extensions are present.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: Other non-profile extensions added to an implementation might alter or conflict with the behavior of the mandatory or optional extensions in a profile, in which case the implementation would not be compatible with the profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: NOTE: Extensions that are released after a given profile is released are by definition non-profile extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: For example, mandatory or optional profile extensions for a new profile might be prototyped as non-profile extensions on an earlier profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

sentences.text: A profile name is a string comprised of, in order:
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: Prefix *RV* for RISC-V. . A specific profile family name string.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: Currently a single letter (*I*, *A*, or *B*), but later profiles may have longer family name strings. . A numeric string giving the first complete calendar year for which the profile is ratified, represented as number of years after year 2000, i.e., *20* for profiles built on specifications ratified during 2019.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: The year string will be longer than two digits in the next century. . A privilege mode (*U*, *S*, *M*).
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: Hypervisor support is treated as an option. . A base ISA XLEN specifier (*32*, *64*).
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: The initial profiles based on specifications ratified in 2019 are:
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: RVI20U32 basic unprivileged instructions for RV32I - RVI20U64 basic unprivileged instructions for RV64I - RVA20U64, RVA20S64 64-bit application-processor profiles
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: NOTE: Profile names are embeddable into RISC-V ISA naming strings.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: This implies that there will be no standard ISA extension with a name that matches the profile naming convention.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: This allows tools that process the RISC-V ISA naming string to parse and/or process a combined string.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

sentences.text: RISC-V was designed to provide a highly modular and extensible instruction set and includes a large and growing set of standard extensions, where each standard extension is a bundle of instruction-set features.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: This is no different than other industry ISAs that continue to add new ISA features.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Unlike other ISAs, however, RISC-V has a broad set of contributors and implementers, and also allows users to add their own custom extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: For some deep embedded markets, highly customized processor configurations are desirable for efficiency, and all software is compiled, ported, and/or developed in-house by the same organization for that specific processor configuration.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: However, for other markets that expect a substantial fraction of software to be delivered to end-customers in binary form, compatibility across multiple implementations from different RISC-V vendors is required.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: The RISC-V International ISA extension ratification process ensures that all processor vendors have agreed to the specification of a standard extension if present.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: However, by themselves, the ISA extension specifications do not guarantee that a certain set of standard extensions will be present in all implementations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: *The primary goal of the RVA profiles is to align processor vendors targeting binary software markets, so software can rely on the existence of a certain set of ISA features in a particular generation of RISC-V implementations.*
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Alignment is not only for compatibility, but also to ensure RISC-V is competitive in these markets.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: The binary app markets are also generally those with the most competitive performance requirements (e.g., mobile, client, server). RISC-V International cannot mandate the ISA features that a RISC-V binary software ecosystem should use, as each ecosystem will typically select the lowest-common denominator they empirically observe in the deployed devices in their target markets.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: But we can align hardware vendors to support a common set of features in each generation through the RVA profiles.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Without proactive alignment through RVA profiles, RISC-V will be uncompetitive, as even if a particular vendor implements a certain feature, if other vendors do not, then binary distributions will not generally use that feature and all implementations will suffer.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: While certain features may be discoverable, and alternate code provided in case of presence/absence of a feature, the added cost to support such options is only justified for certain limited cases, and binary app markets will not support a wide range of optional features, particularly for the nascent RISC-V binary app ecosystems.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: To maintain alignment and increase RISC-V competitiveness over time, the mandatory set of extensions must increase over time in successive generations of RVA profile. (RVA profiles may eventually have to deprecate previously mandatory instructions, but that is unlikely in the near future.) Note that the RISC-V ISA will continue to evolve, regardless of whether a given software ecosystem settles on a certain generation of profile as the baseline for their ecosystem for many years or even decades.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: There are many existing binary software ecosystems, which will migrate to RISC-V and evolve at different rates, and more new ones will doubtless be created over the hopefully long lifetime of RISC-V.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: High-performance application processors require considerable investment, and no single binary app ecosystem can justify the development costs of these processors, especially for RISC-V in its early stage of adoption.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: While the heart of the profile is the set of mandatory extensions, there are several kinds of optional extension that serve important roles in the profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: The first kind are localized options, whose presence or use necessarily differs along geo-political and/or jurisdictional boundaries, with crypto being the obvious example.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: These will always be optional.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: At least for crypto, discovery has been found to be perfectly acceptable to handle this optionality on other architectures, as the use of the extensions is well contained in certain libraries.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: The second kind of optional extension is a development option, which represents a new ISA extension in an early part of its lifecycle but which is intended to become mandatory in a later generation of the RVA profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Processor vendors and software toolchain providers will have varying development schedules, and providing an optional phase in a new extension's lifecycle provides some flexibility while maintaining overall alignment, and is particularly appropriate when hardware or software development for the extension is complex.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Denoting an extension as a development option signals to the community that development should be prioritized for such extensions as they will become mandatory.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: The third kind of optional extension are expansion options, which are those that may have a large implementation cost but are not always needed in a particular platform, and which can be readily handled by discovery.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: These are also intended to remain available as expansion options in future versions of the profile.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Several supervisor-mode extensions fall into this category, e.g., Sv57, which has a notable PPA impact over Sv48 and is not needed on smaller platforms.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Some unprivileged extensions that may fall into this category are possible future matrix extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: These have large implementation costs, and use of matrix instructions can be readily supported with discovery and alternate math libraries.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: The fourth kind of optional extensions are transitory options, where it is not clear if the extension will change to a mandatory, localized, or expansion option, or be possibly dropped over time.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Cryptography provides some examples where earlier cyphers have been broken and are now deprecated.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: We used this mechanism to enable scalar crypto until vector crypto was ready.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Software security features may also be in this category, with examples of deprecated security features occuring in other architectures.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: As another example, the recent avalanche of new numeric datatypes for AI/ML may eventually subside with a few survivors actually being used longer term.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Denoting an option as transitory signals to the community that this extension may be removed in a future profile, though the time scale may span many years.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Except for the localized options, it could be argued that other three kinds of option could be left out of profiles.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: Binary distributions of applications willing to invest in discovery can use an optional extension, and customers compiling their own applications can take advantage of the feature on a particular implementation, even when that system is mostly running binary distributions that ignore the new extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

sentences.text: However, there is value in providing guidance to align hardware vendors and software developers around what extensions are worth implementing and worth discovering, by designating only a few important features as profile options and limiting their granularity.
sentences.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale
